era_context: The early 13th century is characterized by the Mongol expansion under Ögedei Khan, the consolidation of European feudalism and early nation-states, the height of the Crusades, and the flourishing of Islamic and Christian scholasticism. The Jin Dynasty in northern China is under severe pressure from Mongol invasions. In Europe, the papacy is asserting unprecedented authority, and the mendicant orders are expanding their influence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia (sparse documentation in Western sources)
Americas (no written records in Old World sources)

events.title: Treaty of Jaffa (Sixth Crusade settlement)
events.description: Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor, negotiates a treaty with Al-Kamil Muhammad of the Ayyubid Sultanate, securing Christian control of Jerusalem and other holy sites for a period of ten years. This settlement concludes the Sixth Crusade without major military engagement, achieved through diplomatic negotiation rather than warfare. The treaty represents a significant diplomatic victory for Frederick II and demonstrates the declining military capacity of both Crusader and Islamic powers in the Levant.
key_figures: Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor (Crusader leader)

sources.name: Chronicle of the Sixth Crusade (contemporary accounts)

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Jaffa is well-documented in both Christian and Islamic sources, with the date of 1229 firmly established.

events.title: Mongol Siege of Zhongdu (Beijing) region
events.description: Mongol forces under Ögedei Khan continue their systematic conquest of Jin Dynasty territories, with operations in the region around Zhongdu (modern Beijing). The Mongols are consolidating control over northern China and preparing for the final assault on the Jin capital. These campaigns demonstrate the Mongol military machine's efficiency and the Jin Dynasty's declining capacity to resist.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Mongol campaigns is well-documented, though specific events in 1229 may be conflated with adjacent years.

events.title: Reconquista: Siege of Baza
events.description: Christian forces, primarily from Castile, besiege the Islamic fortress of Baza in southern Iberia. The siege represents part of the broader Reconquista campaigns of the 1220s–1230s. While the siege may not have concluded in 1229, it represents the ongoing military pressure exerted by Christian kingdoms against Islamic Al-Andalus.

sources.name: Crónica de los Reyes de Castila (Chronicle of the Kings of Castile)

events.certainty_note: The siege of Baza is documented in later chronicles, but the exact dates are uncertain; it may have occurred in 1228–1230.

events.title: Inquisition activities in southern France
events.description: The newly established Papal Inquisition, staffed primarily by Dominican friars, intensifies its activities against Cathar heretics in southern France. The Inquisition conducts investigations, interrogations, and trials of suspected heretics. This represents the formalization of systematic heresy prosecution and the expansion of papal authority into local ecclesiastical matters.
events.certainty_note: Inquisitorial activities in southern France are well-documented in surviving records from the period.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Jaffa is sometimes interpreted as a diplomatic failure for the papacy, as Frederick II negotiated directly with Islamic powers without papal approval, leading to papal excommunication. The significance of specific military engagements in 1229 (such as the siege of Baza) is difficult to assess due to sparse documentation and conflicting dates in later chronicles. The Inquisition's activities in 1229 are documented but may be overstated in terms of their immediate impact on heresy suppression.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1229 is moderate, with European sources (particularly papal records and crusade chronicles) being relatively rich. East Asian sources depend on later compilations. Islamic sources provide some contemporary accounts of the Treaty of Jaffa and Ayyubid affairs. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Europe and the Levant, with minimal coverage of other regions. Biases in surviving sources favor political and military events over economic, social, or cultural developments. The Treaty of Jaffa is well-documented from both Christian and Islamic perspectives, making it one of the better-attested events of this year.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Jaffa and establishment of the Inquisition represent parallel developments in medieval Christian authority
Mongol campaigns of 1229 set the stage for intensified campaigns in 1231
Inquisitorial activities of 1229 contributed to the formalization of the Inquisition in 1231
